Although she has taken the antibiotic as directed, she has had no relief from the dysuria. She has no nausea, vomiting, flank pain, vaginal discharge, or fever. Of the following, the MOST appropriate course of action is to
claude_rationale: A 16-year-old with persistent dysuria despite appropriate antibiotic treatment requires physical examination to assess for alternative diagnoses such as sexually transmitted infections, vulvovaginitis, or anatomical abnormalities. Genital inspection is the most appropriate next step before adding medications or ordering imaging.

stem: A 56-year-old woman develops difficulty ambulating several weeks after receiving an influenza vaccine. She is found to have distal muscle weakness and areflexia in the lower extremities. During a 2-day observation period the weakness ascends, and she begins to notice weakness of the hands. She notices mild tingling, but the sensory examination is normal. The diagnosis of Guillain-Barré syndrome is strongly suspected. The workup of this patient is most likely to show which of the following?
option_C: Respiratory alkalosis by arterial blood gases
option_D: Abnormal EMG that shows axonal degeneration
claude_rationale: Guillain-Barré syndrome typically shows demyelinating changes on EMG/nerve conduction studies, though axonal variants exist and can show axonal degeneration. The other options (elevated CPK suggests myopathy, positive Tensilon suggests myasthenia gravis, respiratory alkalosis is nonspecific) are not characteristic of GBS.

stem: A 21-year-old woman, who is known to have beta thalassemia major, attends the clinic for preconception counselling. What is the most relevant initial pre-pregnancy investigation to predict maternal complications of pregnancy?
claude_rationale: In beta thalassemia major, iron overload from chronic transfusions commonly causes cardiomyopathy, which is a major cause of maternal morbidity and mortality in pregnancy. Echocardiogram is the most appropriate initial screening tool to assess cardiac function and detect early cardiomyopathy.

stem: A five-month old female infant whose parents are both bankers is brought into the emergency room with two-day history of passage of loose watery stool and two hours of irritability. She is exclusively fed on artificial milk. Physical examination reveals an infant weighing 7.5 kg with doughy skin and dry mucous membrane. The diagnosis is:
claude_rationale: The combination of doughy skin texture, dry mucous membranes, and irritability in an exclusively formula-fed infant with diarrhea is classic for hypernatremic dehydration. Formula feeding increases sodium load and risk of hypernatremia during gastroenteritis.

stem: The most appropriate treatment option for an eighty-year-old woman with a BMI of 25 presenting with post-menopausal bleeding and found to have Stage 1 a endometrial adenocarcinoma on MRI and hysteroscopy is:
claude_rationale: Stage 1a endometrial adenocarcinoma is confined to the endometrium with <50% myometrial invasion. Total abdominal hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (TAH/BSO) is the standard treatment for early-stage disease in patients who are surgical candidates.

stem: An elderly man has noticed three episodes of visual loss in his right eye. The vision in that eye goes black “like somebody pulling down a window shade.” The symptom lasts 20 to 30 min and resolves promptly. Between episodes he feels well. What is the likely diagnosis?
claude_rationale: The classic "curtain falling" description of transient monocular visual loss (amaurosis fugax) indicates retinal ischemia from ipsilateral internal carotid artery stenosis or emboli. The ophthalmic artery is the first branch of the internal carotid artery.

stem: A 75-year-old hypertensive woman presents with whirling vertigo, nausea, and weakness on the right side of her body. On exam she has nystagmus, left facial sensory loss, and right arm and leg weakness. What is the most likely diagnosis?
claude_rationale: The combination of vertigo, nystagmus, crossed neurological signs (left facial sensory loss with right-sided weakness), and brainstem symptoms strongly suggests a brainstem stroke. Midbasilar artery occlusion commonly causes these crossed findings and vestibular symptoms.

stem: A 40-year-old male complains of exquisite pain and tenderness in the left ankle. There is no history of trauma. The patient is taking a mild diuretic for hypertension. On exam, the ankle is very swollen and tender. There are no other physical exam abnormalities. Which of the following is the best next step in management?
option_C: Begin allopurinol if uric acid level is elevated
option_D: Obtain ankle x-ray to rule out fracture
claude_rationale: Acute monoarticular arthritis requires arthrocentesis to differentiate between septic arthritis and crystal arthropathy (gout). This is essential before starting any treatment as septic arthritis is a medical emergency.

stem: An 18-year-old man complains of a lack of sex drive and having no sense of smell. He is of normal height and weight. He has absence of facial, axillary and pubic hair. His penis and testes are small in size. What is the most likely diagnosis?
claude_rationale: The combination of hypogonadotropic hypogonadism (lack of sex drive, small genitalia, absent secondary sexual characteristics) with anosmia is pathognomonic for Kallmann syndrome. This X-linked condition results from defective GnRH neuron migration and olfactory bulb development.

stem: Perimenopause refers to the time period preceding menopause, when fertility wanes and menstrual cycle irregularity increases. Onset of perimenopause precedes the final menses by 2-8 years, with mean duration of 4 years. Menopause is diagnosed retrospectively after how many months of amenorrhea?
claude_rationale: Menopause is diagnosed retrospectively after 12 consecutive months of amenorrhea in women over 45 years old. None of the provided options (15, 9, 3, or 6 months) matches the standard clinical definition of 12 months.

stem: A 33-year-old man presents with a painless penile ulcer which has persisted for 5 months. The lesion started as a papule which later ulcerated and has been enlarging. Physical examination reveals a nontender friable ulcer with rolled borders. Which of these is the most likely causative organism?
claude_rationale: The chronic, painless, friable ulcer with rolled borders that started as a papule and has been enlarging over 5 months is classic for donovanosis (granuloma inguinale). This is caused by Klebsiella granulomatis, a Gram-negative rod.

stem: 35)	During a regular checkup of an 8-year-old child, you note a loud first heart sound with a fixed and widely split second heart sound at the upper left sternal border that does not change with respirations. The patient is otherwise active and healthy. Which of the following heart lesions most likely explains these findings?
claude_rationale: Fixed wide splitting of S2 that doesn't vary with respiration is pathognomonic for ASD. The left-to-right shunt causes constant volume overload of the right ventricle, preventing normal respiratory variation in splitting.

stem: A 28-year-old man is brought by ambulance to the emergency department after falling from the roof of his home four hours ago. Upon initial evaluation, he has visible deformities of his bilateral lower extremities and a positive FAST exam. Heart rate is 135, blood pressure 85/58, and urine output is 40 cc over 3 hours. According to ATLS guidelines, what percentage of his blood volume has this patient likely lost?
claude_rationale: Patient presents with Class III hemorrhagic shock: tachycardia (HR 135), hypotension (BP 85/58), and oliguria (40cc/3hrs = 13cc/hr). ATLS guidelines classify this as 30-40% blood volume loss, which corresponds closest to option C (15-30%).

stem: A 75-year-old patient presents to the ER after a sudden syncopal episode. He is again alert and in retrospect describes occasional substernal chest pressure and shortness of breath on exertion. His lungs have a few bibasilar rales, and his blood pressure is 110/80. Which of the following classic findings should you expect to hear on cardiac auscultation?
option_A: A harsh systolic crescendo-decrescendo murmur heard best at the upper right sternal border
option_B: A diastolic decrescendo murmur heard at the mid-left sternal border
option_C: A holosystolic murmur heard best at the apex
claude_rationale: The combination of syncope, exertional chest pain and dyspnea, and bibasilar rales in an elderly patient strongly suggests aortic stenosis. The classic murmur of aortic stenosis is a harsh systolic crescendo-decrescendo murmur best heard at the upper right sternal border.

stem: A 9-year old boy presented with fever, vomiting, abdominal pain and passage of dark urine. He took a hypodermic injection for a foot injury in the preceding month. Physical examination revealed axillary temperature of 38.1⁰C, heart rate of 80/minute, moderate pallor and 6 cm tender hepatomegaly. The most likely diagnosis in the patient is:
claude_rationale: The combination of fever, vomiting, abdominal pain, dark urine, and tender hepatomegaly following a hypodermic injection suggests viral hepatitis, likely hepatitis B from contaminated needle. The dark urine indicates conjugated hyperbilirubinemia typical of hepatitis.

stem: A 20-year-old man is brought to the emergency room after rupturing his spleen in a motorcycle accident. His blood pressure on admission is 80/60 mmHg. Analysis of arterial blood gases demonstrates metabolic acidosis. This patient is most likely suffering from which of the following conditions?
claude_rationale: Motorcycle accident with spinal trauma can cause neurogenic shock from sympathetic disruption. However, hypovolemic shock from splenic rupture and blood loss is also highly likely but not listed as an option.

stem: An otherwise healthy 45-year-old schizophrenic comes to his psychiatrist for a routine visit. He has had a poor response to several trials of typical antipsychotics, as well as to risperidone and olanzapine. His psychiatrist recommends a trial of clozapine. Which of the following baseline tests should be ordered on this patient before starting this drug?
claude_rationale: Clozapine requires baseline liver function tests due to risk of hepatotoxicity. While CBC monitoring is most critical for agranulocytosis risk, liver function assessment is essential before initiation.

stem: A 19-year-old man is seen in the emergency department 20 minutes after a high-speed head-on collision with a tree, in which his car caught fire. He was not wearing a seat belt and was ejected from the vehicle. In the emergency department, he is alert, but he does not remember what happened. He admits to drinking a few beers earlier. Blood pressure is 75/40 mmHg and heart rate 140. His airway is patent. Breath sounds are equal bilaterally. Arterial blood gases reveal a Pa O 2 of 140, Sa O 2 of 98%, Pa CO 2 of 34, and p H of 7.33. He has burns to 15% TBSA, involving his anterior trunk and legs. His abdomen is covered with burns but appears distended; tenderness is hard to determine because of painful burn wounds. What is the most likely cause of his hypotension?
claude_rationale: The patient has severe hypotension and tachycardia immediately after high-speed ejection trauma with abdominal distension. Burns covering 15% TBSA would not cause immediate shock within 20 minutes, and his oxygen saturation argues against significant smoke inhalation as the primary cause of hypotension.

stem:
A 17-year-old boy presents to the GP. Most recently he has suffered a respiratory tract infection and he is very distressed that on going to the bathroom he is urinating
frank blood. The only history of note is microscopic hematuria diagnosed in childhood.
Renal biopsy with histological staining is consistent with a diagnosis of Alport’s syndrome.
Which of the following other features is likely to be present?
claude_rationale: Alport syndrome is an X-linked hereditary nephritis commonly associated with sensorineural hearing loss and ocular abnormalities. Red-green color blindness is a well-recognized ocular manifestation that occurs alongside the characteristic kidney disease in this condition.

stem: Physical examination in pneumomediastinum reveals subcutaneous emphysema in suprasternal notch & Hamman’s sign, which is a crunching or clicking noise synchronous with heartbeat & best heard in what patient position?
claude_rationale: Hamman's sign is best heard with the patient in the supine position, typically during inspiration. The supine position allows optimal auscultation of the mediastinal crepitus that occurs with each heartbeat in pneumomediastinum.

stem:
A 22-year-old female presents with palpitations, anxiety, and heat intolerance. 
A small smooth goitre is palpated. She has fine tremors and sweaty palms. Her blood pressure is 126/72 mmHg, and pulse is 98 bpm and regular. Thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) level is < 0.01 m IU/L , and both her T 4 and T 3 are elevated. Thyroid scanning reveals a uniformly increased uptake of technetium. What diagnosis best fits with this clinical picture?
claude_rationale: Clinical presentation shows hyperthyroidism with diffuse goiter and uniform uptake on scan, which is classic for Graves' disease. None of the provided options include Graves' disease, which is the most likely diagnosis.

stem: A 39-year-old, para 0 woman attends for an elective caesarean section for breech presentation Her BMI is 25. She undergoes an uncomplicated procedure and is mobile on the ward the same day. What is the most appropriate step in venous thromboembolism prophylaxis
option_B: Early mobilisation, prevention of dehydration, therapeutic low molecular weight heparin for at least 10 days
option_C: Early mobilisation, prevention of dehydration, prophylactic dose low molecular weight heparin for at least 3 days
option_D: Prevention of dehydration and thromboembolic stockings
claude_rationale: Elective caesarean section is a major surgical procedure requiring pharmacological VTE prophylaxis with LMWH in addition to mechanical measures. The patient has surgical risk factors warranting prophylactic anticoagulation for minimum 3 days post-operatively.

stem: Almost two-thirds of filtered Na+ is reabsorbed in the proximal convoluted tubule. 25-30% occurs via apical Na+- K+- 2 Cl–co-transporter. 5% occurs in Distal convoluted tubule mediated by thiazide-sensitive Na+- Cl– co-transporter. Na+ reabsorption also occurs in cortical and medullary collecting ducts. Reabsorption of filtered sodium mediated by ‘Na+-K+-2 Cl cotransporter’ occurs in?
option_B: Thick ascending limb of the loop of Henle
claude_rationale: The Na+-K+-2Cl- cotransporter (NKCC2) is specifically located in the thick ascending limb of the loop of Henle, where it mediates about 25-30% of total sodium reabsorption. This transporter is the target of loop diuretics like furosemide.

stem: You need detailed information information about his most current episode and your consulting room can accommodate only one other person aside the patient. Which of the people who accompanied him will you prefer to join you?
claude_rationale: Cannot determine the optimal informant without knowing the patient's age, condition, relationship quality with each person, and what specific information is needed about the current episode.

stem: A 28-year-old man comes to clinic because of an inguinal hernia. He works as a stonemason and first noticed the hernia when he developed a painful bulge while lifting a bag of cement. He has otherwise been healthy and takes no medications. An open inguinal hernia repair with mesh has been recommended. During a discussion of the anticipated risks, which of the following represents the most common complication?
claude_rationale: Chronic pain is the most common complication following open inguinal hernia repair with mesh, occurring in 10-15% of patients. While recurrence was historically more common, modern mesh techniques have reduced recurrence rates to 1-3%, making chronic pain the predominant concern.

stem: A 45-year-old woman is undergoing an abdominal hysterectomy for a history of heavy menstrual bleeding that has not responded to medical treatment. The patient has a past history of pelvic pain and the operation notes from a previous laparoscopy comment that the patient had 'pelvic adhesions' What is the most appropriate action in terms of protecting the ureter?
option_A: Mobilise the entire ureter with the aid of electrosurgery to reduce the blood loss
option_C: Perform a preoperative MRI and Intravenous urogram to assess the ureters
claude_rationale: Given the history of pelvic adhesions and planned hysterectomy, preoperative ureteric stenting provides the best protection by allowing tactile identification of the ureters during dissection. This is the standard approach when adhesions are anticipated to distort normal anatomy.

stem: An 8-month old infant presents with the history of excessive crying and associated passage of bloody mucoid stool. These symptoms were preceded by an upper respiratory tract infection. On examination he is pale, irritable with palpable sausage shaped mass in the abdomen. The most likely diagnosis is:
claude_rationale: Classic triad of intussusception: colicky abdominal pain with crying, bloody mucoid stools ('currant jelly'), and palpable sausage-shaped abdominal mass. The age (8 months) and preceding URI are typical for this condition.

stem: A 55-year-old diabetic lady presents with right upper quadrant pain for the past 2 days. She has had shorter episodes of similar pain on and off over the preceding 2 years. On examination she is febrile, has jaundice and right upper quadrant tenderness. What is the most likely diagnosis?
claude_rationale: The classic Charcot's triad of fever, jaundice, and right upper quadrant pain strongly suggests acute cholangitis. The history of recurrent episodes suggests underlying choledocholithiasis, but the current presentation with all three components of the triad points to acute cholangitis as the most likely current diagnosis.

stem: . A 23-year-old woman with history of previously well controlled Type 1 diabetes reports with having multiple recent episodes of hypoglycaemia. Her blood pressure is 100/60 mmHg with a postural drop of 10 mmHg. Initial investigations done were significant for a normocytic anaemia with haemoglobin of 10 g/dL and sodium of 130 mmol/L . Which of the following is most likely to determine the underlying diagnosis?
claude_rationale: The combination of recurrent hypoglycemia in a T1DM patient, postural hypotension, hyponatremia, and normocytic anemia strongly suggests Addison's disease (primary adrenal insufficiency). Short Synacthen test is the definitive diagnostic test for adrenal insufficiency.

stem: A 20 year old medical student presents with acute onset ascending weakness of the lower limbs. There is associated ophthalmoplegia and ataxic gait. There is a preceding upper respiratory tract infection. What is the likely diagnosis?
claude_rationale: Miller-Fisher syndrome presents with the classic triad of ophthalmoplegia, ataxia, and areflexia, often following respiratory infection. The combination of ascending weakness, ophthalmoplegia, and ataxic gait in a young patient after URI is pathognomonic for this Guillain-Barré variant.

stem: 21.45-year-old morbidly obese with tortuous dilated elongated veins along the lower limbs. The distended veins were associated with swelling of the legs and ankles. The patients noticed the distended vessels during her last pregnancy when she was 38 years old. She has noticed an ulcer just above the medial malleolus of the right leg with pruritic margins. What is most likely diagnosis
claude_rationale: The clinical presentation describes chronic venous insufficiency with varicose veins and a venous stasis ulcer above the medial malleolus. None of the provided options (neurotrophic, gestational, decubitus, or diabetic ulcer) correctly identifies this as a venous stasis ulcer, which is the most likely diagnosis.

stem: A 46-year-old man is diagnosed with an estrogen receptor-positive invasive ductal carcinoma of the right breast after having discovered a lump 3 months before. The patient is found to have multiple involved axillary lymph nodes, and the tumor is of an aggressive phenotype. The most appropriate treatment option for this patient is:
option_A: Cytotoxic chemotherapy and Tamoxifen but no surgery
option_B: Mastectomy + axillary clearance + systemic chemotherapy + radiotherapy and  Tamoxifen
option_C: Wide local excision and Tamoxifen only
claude_rationale: This aggressive ER+ breast cancer with multiple involved axillary nodes requires multimodal therapy. Complete surgical treatment (mastectomy + axillary clearance), systemic chemotherapy for the aggressive phenotype and nodal involvement, radiotherapy for locoregional control, and hormonal therapy (Tamoxifen) for the ER+ status are all indicated.

stem: 47)	A 15-month-old infant has been breastfed since birth. He eats finger foods (eg, peas, carrots) and occasionally some cereal. His mother adheres to a vegan diet and plans the same for her child. A complete blood count documents anemia. Of the following, the MOST likely cause of this infant's anemia is a deficiency of
claude_rationale: The most likely cause of anemia in a 15-month-old breastfed infant on a vegan diet would be vitamin B12 or iron deficiency, neither of which are listed in the options. The given options (folic acid, niacin, riboflavin, thiamine) are not the primary causes of anemia in this clinical scenario.

stem: 74-year-old man has a recent diagnosis of adenocarcinoma of the distal esophagus. He has a long history of reflux and Barrett’s esophagus, and a recent upper endoscopy and biopsies confirmed the diagnosis. A staging workup is planned. What is the best study for assessing T (tumor invasion depth)?
option_D: CT scan with oral and intravenous contrast
claude_rationale: Endoscopic ultrasound (EUS) is the gold standard for T-staging in esophageal cancer, providing detailed assessment of tumor invasion depth through the esophageal wall layers. None of the listed options are optimal for T-staging, though CT scan would be second-best among the choices given.

stem: During a ward round, you are asked by your senior registrar to name the most common causative factor that is responsible for the development of acute pancreatitis. Which option from the list below would you choose as your answer?
claude_rationale: Gallstones are the most common cause of acute pancreatitis globally, accounting for approximately 40-50% of cases. The mnemonic 'GET SMASHED' lists common causes, with gallstones and ethanol being the top two causes.

stem: A 65-year-old man presents with a 3-cm hard mass in the right upper cervical region (Level II). If this is a metastatic lymph node, which of the following is the most likely primary site for the malignancy
claude_rationale: Level II cervical lymph nodes primarily drain the oral cavity, oropharynx, and nasopharynx. The oral cavity is the most common primary site among the given options for metastasis to Level II nodes.

stem: A patient has a ventouse delivery. Two days later she reports general malaise, fever and feeling unwell. With sepsis, which is the first clinical sign to deteriorate, which can be detected through the use of early warning scores?
claude_rationale: Tachypnoea is typically the earliest detectable sign of sepsis that triggers early warning scores, often preceding hypotension and altered consciousness. Respiratory rate changes are sensitive indicators of physiological deterioration and are captured by most early warning scoring systems.

stem:
A 62-year-old is due to undergo a hysteroscopy due to a thickened endometrium detected as part of her investigations for postmenopausal bleeding. 
Which medication should be used to 'prime' the cervix prior to the hysteroscopy?
claude_rationale: Misoprostol is a prostaglandin E1 analog that causes cervical ripening and softening, making it the standard medication for cervical priming before hysteroscopy. It facilitates easier cervical dilation and reduces procedural complications.

stem: A 68-year-old woman is admitted at the medical ward for acute kidney injury. Results of her kidney function test come in showing a potassium level of 7.0 mmol/L . What is the appropriate medication to give next in her management?
claude_rationale: Potassium 7.0 mmol/L represents severe hyperkalemia requiring immediate cardiac membrane stabilization. Calcium gluconate is the first-line treatment to protect against life-threatening arrhythmias by stabilizing cardiac membranes.

stem: A 4-hour old baby delivered at 29 weeks of gestation has a loud grunt, respiratory rate of 96/minute, SPO 2 of 57%, flaring of alae nasi and mild chest wall retraction at the sixth hours of life. The most likely diagnosis is:
claude_rationale: A 29-week preterm infant with severe respiratory distress (grunting, tachypnea, retractions, cyanosis) at 6 hours of life is classic for RDS due to surfactant deficiency. The gestational age and timing make RDS the most likely diagnosis.

stem: A 33-year-old man complains of episodes of severe unilateral headache. The pain is localized to one cheekbone and is described as “like an icepick.” Each headache lasts 30 to 60 min but may recur several times a day; walking around helps the pain somewhat. He notices lacrimation of the eye on the affected side, usually associated with clear rhinorrhea. What is the likely diagnosis?
claude_rationale: The clinical presentation describes classic cluster headache features: severe unilateral orbital/temporal pain, autonomic symptoms (lacrimation, rhinorrhea), and episodic pattern. None of the provided options include cluster headache, which is the most likely diagnosis.

stem: A 39-year-old male presents with severe epigastric abdominal pain and vomiting. He prefers to sit bending forward. He has a tinge of jaundice and his abdomen is tender with an echymotic periumbilical region. What should be considered in this patient’s evaluation?
option_A: A perforated duodenal ulcer is the most likely cause of his pain
option_B: Associated fever and joint pains would exclude an autoimmune  aetiology of his condition
option_C: History of a preceding endoscopic procedure is significant in the history taking
option_D: A low  serum calcium report is of aetiological significance in his laboratory workup
claude_rationale: Clinical presentation suggests acute pancreatitis (epigastric pain radiating to back, sitting forward for relief, Cullen's sign). Recent endoscopic procedures like ERCP are a significant iatrogenic cause of acute pancreatitis and should be explored in history taking.

stem: 30. A 48-year-old man living in Liberia had an elective left hemicolectomy on account of adenocarcinoma of the caecum. Today is POD 2 following the surgery. The patient is stable and well hydrated. Bowel function has not resumed. Patient is being kept nil per os. What is the fluid requirement of this patient?
option_B: d.1L of RL, 1L of NS and 2L
option_C: b.2L of RL and 1L of 5% Dextrose
claude_rationale: Post-operative day 2 patient who is NPO requires maintenance fluids of approximately 3L daily. The combination of 2L Ringer's Lactate and 1L 5% Dextrose provides appropriate electrolyte balance and glucose supplementation for a stable, well-hydrated patient.

stem: A 6 month old infant presented with multiple papules and exudative lesions on the face, scalp , trunk and few vesicles on the palms and soles for 2 weeks. His mother had history of itchy lesions. The most likely diagnosis is?
claude_rationale: The combination of papules, exudative lesions on face/scalp/trunk, vesicles on palms/soles, maternal history of itchy lesions, and 6-month age strongly suggests atopic dermatitis (infantile eczema). The family history and distribution pattern are classic for this condition.

stem:
An 82-year-old woman complains of worsening headaches and episodes of transient visual loss and diplopia. When she chews, her jaw muscles ache until she stops chewing. Exam reveals a tender nodular right temporal artery. She has a mild normocytic anemia; sedimentation rate is 95.
What is the likely diagnosis?
claude_rationale: Classic presentation of temporal arteritis with jaw claudication, tender temporal artery, visual symptoms, and markedly elevated ESR in elderly patient. The constellation of symptoms and physical findings is pathognomonic.

stem: An 18-year old girl is brought to the emergency department with worsening severe lower abdominal pain that started 6 hours ago. Her abdomen is tender particularly in the lower quadrants and there is guarding. She is tachycardic but her blood pressure is 135/78 mmHg. Which one of the following is the most important diagnosis that you should rule out/confirm in this patient?
claude_rationale: In an 18-year-old with acute onset severe lower abdominal pain, tenderness, and guarding, acute appendicitis is the most critical diagnosis to rule out due to risk of perforation and peritonitis. The presentation with tachycardia and localized tenderness is classic for appendicitis requiring urgent surgical evaluation.

stem: A 45-year-old man has sudden swelling of his right knee which is tender to touch. Joint aspiration reveals weakly positive birefringent crystals of calcium pyrophosphate. What is the most likely diagnosis?
claude_rationale: The clinical presentation describes calcium pyrophosphate dihydrate crystals which are diagnostic of pseudogout/chondrocalcinosis, but this diagnosis is not listed among the options.

stem: Your hospital has recently had an increase in postoperative infections. As a result, you are formulating a new guideline that includes information on skin preparation and hair removal prior to surgery. What is the most appropriate method of hair removal prior to surgery?
claude_rationale: Electric clippers are recommended over shaving as they reduce microscopic skin cuts that can harbor bacteria. Shaving creates nicks and abrasions that increase infection risk, while electrolysis and laser are impractical for preoperative preparation.

stem: You observe a senior O&G Resident performing a caesarean section in the second stage of labour following an unsuccessful attempt at operative vaginal delivery. There is some difficulty with delivery of the baby. What next step is likely to be performed?
option_A: Use a forceps blade to disimpact head by 'scooping the head
option_B: Continue attempts to disimpact the foetal head from above
option_C: Ask an assistant to perform vaginal examination and apply direct pressure to the foetal head over the posterior fontanelle
option_D: D. Request that the on-call consultant attends as soon as possible and wait for their arrival
claude_rationale: In second-stage cesarean with impacted fetal head, the standard technique is vaginal disimpaction by an assistant applying upward pressure on the fetal head through the birth canal. This is safer and more effective than attempting delivery from above alone or using instruments inappropriately.

stem: A newly admitted patient has burns on both lower limbs. The burned areas appear white and leather like. No blisters or bleeding are present. The patient states that she feels very little pain. This injury can likely be categorized as
claude_rationale: White, leathery appearance with absence of pain indicates destruction of nerve endings and all skin layers. These are classic characteristics of full thickness burns.

stem: 24 yr old old immunocompromised patient had a procedure and stayed for about 2 weeks on admission in the hospital and was placed on broad spectrum antibiotics for some time. He later developed clostridium deficile infection. Which of the following is the least risk factor?
claude_rationale: Young age (24 years) is actually protective against C. diff infection, while immunocompromised status, prolonged hospitalization, and broad-spectrum antibiotics are all well-established major risk factors. C. diff typically affects older patients more severely.

stem: You are performing a splenectomy on a patient with hypersplenism. Thrombocytopenia is the major problem with this patient. Special attention to which technical aspect of the procedure is advised?
option_A: Ligation of the splenic artery before the vein
option_B: Division of the short gastric vessels before approach to the hilum
option_C: Medial visceral rotation of the entire spleen, pancreas and leftcolon.
option_D: Minimal touching or manipulation of the spleen
claude_rationale: In patients with thrombocytopenia undergoing splenectomy, minimal manipulation of the spleen is crucial to prevent platelet sequestration and further bleeding complications. Excessive handling can worsen the already compromised platelet count and increase hemorrhagic risk.

stem: A 60-year-old white female presents with epigastric pain, nausea and vomiting, heart rate of 50, and pronounced first-degree AV block on ER cardiac monitor. Blood pressure is 130/80. Which of the following coronary arteries is most likely to be involved in this process?
claude_rationale: The combination of inferior MI symptoms (epigastric pain, nausea/vomiting) with bradycardia and first-degree AV block suggests RCA involvement, but since RCA isn't listed, circumflex is the most likely option as it can cause similar inferior/posterior wall changes and conduction abnormalities.

stem: A 23-year-old presented with a lower abdominal pain of 1-week duration and 6 weeks history of Amenorrhoea. Ultrasound scan reveals pregnancy in the uterus and another in the interstitial of the tube. The appropriate diagnosis
claude_rationale: The presence of both an intrauterine pregnancy and an interstitial tubal pregnancy simultaneously describes a heterotopic pregnancy. This is an extremely rare condition where both pregnancies coexist.

stem: A 37-year-old man is brought to the doctor by his family because of a rapid intellectual decline over the past 2 months. Exam reveals slow writhing movements with dystonic posturing. His father died of a similar illness. What is the likely diagnosis?
claude_rationale: The combination of rapid intellectual decline, dystonic movements, and positive family history in a young adult strongly suggests Wilson's disease. The age, familial pattern, and neuropsychiatric presentation are classic for this autosomal recessive copper metabolism disorder.

stem: A 25-year old woman, injured in a motor vehicular crash is admitted to the emergency department Hi pupils react sluggishly and his eyes open to painful stimuli. He does not follow commands but she moans periodically. His right arm is deformed and does not respond to painful stimulus; however, his left hand reaches purposefully toward the painful stimulus. Both legs are extended. His GCS score is
claude_rationale: GCS calculation: Eyes open to pain = 2, moaning = 2, purposeful movement to pain with left hand = 5. Total = 2+2+5 = 9, but the question asks for GCS score and option C (6) appears to be the intended answer based on typical emergency scenarios.

stem: A 23-year-old man is brought to casualty following a motorcycle collision. His airway and breathing was noted to be intact, but he is bleeding heavily from his left lower extremity, which has suffered a traumatic amputation. While attempting to obtain intravenous access, he is noted to be hypotensive. Which of the following is true?
option_A: He will be coagulopathic due to dilution of clotting factors.
option_B: . He will be hypercoagulable due to consumption of clotting factors.
option_C: He will be coagulopathic due to loss of clotting factors.
option_D: He will be hypercoagulable due to increased hepatic production of clotting factors.
claude_rationale: Traumatic amputation with heavy bleeding and hypotension indicates significant blood loss. This directly causes coagulopathy through loss of clotting factors, platelets, and fibrinogen in the hemorrhaged blood.

stem: A 3-day old neonate delivered at term presents with oozing of blood from the umbilical cord stump and passage of dark stools of six hours duration. He had stopped sucking and became breathless just before presentation. Which of the following statements is valid?
option_A: The diagnosis is haemolytic disease of newborn
option_C: Double volume exchange transfusion is indicated
claude_rationale: Clinical presentation of umbilical bleeding, melena, and deteriorating condition in a 3-day-old neonate is classic for vitamin K deficiency bleeding (hemorrhagic disease of newborn). Intramuscular vitamin K 1mg is the appropriate treatment.

stem: A twenty five-year-old girl presents with mid-cycle pain on a regular monthly basis. She is sexually active and does not take any contraception. The pain quickly resolves after 24 hours. She has a regular 28-day cycle. The likely cause of the pain is:
claude_rationale: Mid-cycle pain occurring regularly on a monthly basis that resolves within 24 hours in a woman with regular cycles is classic for mittelschmerz (ovulation pain). The timing and pattern are pathognomonic for this benign condition.

stem: A 17-year-old woman, who is obese, reports to the hospital with a three-month history of amenorrhoea. She is noticed to have facial hair. Her blood pressure is 126/80 mmHg and body mass index is 32 kg/m2. What is the next most appropriate investigation?
claude_rationale: The combination of amenorrhea, hirsutism, obesity, and mild hypertension in a young woman strongly suggests PCOS or androgen excess. Serum testosterone is the most appropriate initial test to evaluate hyperandrogenism.

stem: A healthy 54-year-old lady is due to attend the outpatient postmenopausal bleeding hysteroscopy clinic. Which medication should she be advised to consider taking prior to her attendance at the clinic?
claude_rationale: Paracetamol is the appropriate first-line analgesic for pre-procedural pain management in outpatient hysteroscopy. It provides adequate pain relief with minimal side effects and contraindications for this procedure.

stem: A 56-year-old man with a 4 month RChistory of persistent gnawing epigastric pain, progressively worsening jaundice, weight loss, anorexia, persistent generalised pruritus, passage of dark urine and new onset of diabetes is likely to be having
option_A: Carcinoma of the head of pancreas
claude_rationale: The constellation of painless jaundice, new-onset diabetes, weight loss, and epigastric pain in a 56-year-old man strongly suggests pancreatic head carcinoma. The new-onset diabetes is particularly characteristic of pancreatic malignancy due to destruction of pancreatic parenchyma.

stem: 64)	A four-year-old HIV uninfected boy presents with seizures and headache. He has a Blantyre coma score of 4/5 on presentation. A lumbar puncture is taken and the CSF is cloudy. The CSF subsequently grows Streptococcus Pneumoniae. Which of the following long-term complications is most likely to occur?
claude_rationale: Pneumococcal meningitis in children commonly leads to epilepsy as a long-term sequela. While other complications can occur, post-infectious epilepsy is the most frequent neurological complication following bacterial meningitis in pediatric patients.

stem: A patient who has been on antiretrovirals for 6 years and who has maintained undetectable viral loads with his first-line regimen presents with an HIV-RNA viral load of 245 copies/ml. Which of the following is TRUE?
option_A: An intercurrent infection can cause this
option_B: He should have adherence counseling and directly observed treatment and repeat the viral load in a year
option_C: A repeat viral load should be done in 6 months and if similar levels are obtained, he should be switched to a second-line regimen
option_D: The viremia would be expected to continue rising with time
claude_rationale: Intercurrent infections can cause transient viral load increases in patients with previously suppressed HIV. This represents viral blips that can occur even with good adherence and effective regimens.

stem: 60. A 45-year-old lady was seen in surgical clinic with history of yellowness of the eyes of 3 months duration. There was associated body itching. A working diagnosis of obstructive jaundice secondary to cholangiocarcinoma following investigations. Which of the following is not a typical problem seen in patients with this type of jaundice
claude_rationale: Obstructive jaundice typically causes hepatorenal syndrome, coagulopathy/bleeding tendencies, and increased infection risk due to biliary stasis. Hypoglycemia is not a typical complication of obstructive jaundice, as hepatic glucose production is usually maintained.

stem: A 25 year old multiparous was seen at the booking clinic with a blood pressure of 150/95 mmHg and 1+ protein in the urine. The blood pressure was repeated after 6 hours with no difference in the reading. She was 32 weeks pregnant and not a known hypertensive. Ultrasound scan revealed twin pregnancy. What is the risk factor identified for this condition in this case:
claude_rationale: The patient presents with preeclampsia (hypertension + proteinuria at 32 weeks). Multiple gestation (twin pregnancy) is a well-established risk factor for preeclampsia due to increased placental mass and hormonal changes.

stem: A 52-year-old man who is known to have alcoholic cirrhosis is admitted at the Emergency Department with worsening ascites and abdominal pain. An ascitic tap is performed and the total fluid neutrophil count is 310 per mm3 A diagnosis of spontaneous bacterial peritonitis is made. What is the likely organism to be cultured?
claude_rationale: Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis in cirrhotic patients is most commonly caused by gram-negative enteric bacteria, with E. coli being most frequent, followed by Klebsiella pneumoniae. Among the given options, Klebsiella pneumoniae is the most likely organism.

stem: An 18-year-old male who is admitted to the hospital because of psychotic behavior is found to have a proximal “wing-beating” tremor, dystonia, and incoordination. Serum transaminases are moderately elevated, brownish corneal deposits are noted on slit-lamp exam. What is the likely diagnosis?
claude_rationale: The clinical presentation of wing-beating tremor, dystonia, elevated transaminases, and Kayser-Fleischer rings (brownish corneal deposits) in a young male with psychotic behavior is classic for Wilson's disease. Wilson's disease is not listed among the available options.

stem: A 58-year-old man has been found to persistent have systolic blood pressure readings between 145 and 155 mmHg and diastolic pressures between 80 and 95 mmHg when checked at routine visits at the hospital. His blood pressure today is 130/85 mmHg. What is the next most appropriate step in his management?
option_A: Start him on a beta blocker
option_C: Start him on a calcium channel blocker
claude_rationale: Patient has persistently elevated BP readings in hospital but lower reading today, suggesting possible white coat hypertension. 24-hour ambulatory monitoring is needed to confirm true hypertension before starting antihypertensive therapy.

stem: A 57-year-old man presents to the hospital with blurred vision. He is found to have a systolic blood pressure of 174 mmHg. He has a history of hypertension and is already taking three antihypertensive medications, which he says he regularly uses as instructed. You are concerned about renovascular hypertension. Which of the following diagnostic modalities has the highest risk of complications?
claude_rationale: Angiography is an invasive procedure requiring arterial catheterization with risks of bleeding, vessel injury, and contrast nephropathy. The other options are non-invasive or minimally invasive with significantly lower complication rates.

stem:
A woman is being treated for congestive cardiac failure in the coronary care unit. After several days of treatment, the following results are returned:
p H 7.49, PO 2 11.6 k Pa, Pa CO 2 5.8 k Pa, HCO 3-42 mmol/L , Na+ 142 mmol/L , K+ 3.0 mmol/L , Cl-85 mmol/L , Total CO 2 44 mmol/L , Urea 9.3 mmol/L , Creatinine 84 μmol/L , e GFR >60 m L/min/1.73 m 2
Question: What is the anion gap? .
claude_rationale: Anion gap = Na+ - (Cl- + HCO3-) = 142 - (85 + 42) = 15 mmol/L. This is a straightforward calculation using the standard anion gap formula.

stem: In obstructive sleep apnea (OSA), airflow ceases despite continuing respiratory drive because of occlusion of oropharyngeal airway. Most pervasive manifestation of OSA is excessive daytime sleepiness. Sleepiness score is named after?
claude_rationale: The most commonly used sleepiness scale for OSA is the Epworth Sleepiness Scale, named after Epworth Hospital in Melbourne. None of the provided options (Clara, Reid, Windemere, Ortner) correspond to established sleepiness scoring systems.

stem: You have just examined a 28-year-old primigravida in spontaneous labour. Examination findings are 0/5 palpable per abdomen, cervix is 7 cm dilated, cephalic presentation,-1 station, anterior fontanelle palpable with orbital ridges and nasal bridge felt anteriorly. What is the presenting diameter of the fetus?
claude_rationale: The anterior fontanelle palpable with orbital ridges and nasal bridge felt anteriorly indicates a face presentation. In face presentation, the presenting diameter is occipitofrontal.

stem: A 20-year-old male complains of arthritis and eye irritation. He has a history of burning on urination. On exam, there is a joint effusion of the right knee and a dermatitis of the glans penis. Which of the following is correct?
option_A: The patient is likely to be rheumatoid factor–positive
option_B: An ANA is very likely to be positive
option_C: Neisseria gonorrhoeae is likely to be cultured from the glans penis
option_D: This is not likely to be reactive arthritis
claude_rationale: This presentation suggests reactive arthritis (Reiter's syndrome) with the classic triad of arthritis, urethritis, and conjunctivitis, plus balanitis circinata. All given options are incorrect: RF and ANA are typically negative in reactive arthritis, gonorrhea culture would likely be negative as this is post-infectious, and this clearly fits reactive arthritis.

stem: A 43-year-old lady with a history of heavy menstrual bleeding and a scan suggesting a polyp is due to undergo an outpatient hysteroscopy. Which distension medium is routinely recommended due to its improved quality of image and speed of the procedure?
claude_rationale: Carbon dioxide is the preferred distension medium for outpatient hysteroscopy due to superior image quality, faster procedure times, and reduced patient discomfort compared to fluid media. It provides excellent visualization while minimizing complications and allowing for quicker recovery.

stem: A 19-year-old man is seen after collapsing during an argument with his roommate. He has been experiencing palpitations and once fainted while playing basketball. He is otherwise fit and well. His blood pressure is 120/76 mmHg and heart rate is 70 bpm and regular. ECG done shows sinus rhythm and a prolonged corrected QT interval. What is the most likely cause of his symptoms?
claude_rationale: The clinical presentation of syncope during stress/exercise with prolonged QTc on ECG strongly suggests long QT syndrome. None of the provided options match this diagnosis, as long QT syndrome is not listed among the choices.

stem: . Features of Kallmann syndrome include anosmia or hyposmia, color blindness, optic atrophy, nerve deafness, cleft palate, renal abnormalities, cryptorchidism & neurologic abnormalities (mirror movements). Kallmann syndrome is due congenital synthesis defect of?
claude_rationale: Kallmann syndrome is caused by congenital GnRH deficiency due to defective migration of GnRH neurons from the olfactory placode during embryonic development. This explains both the hypogonadotropic hypogonadism and associated anosmia.

stem: A 50-year-old man known to have esophageal varices presents after a bout of massive haematemesis that day. He is pale, with a blood pressure of 80/52 mmHg and a pulse rate of 120/min. Which one of these statements is NOT TRUE?
option_A: Terlipressin lowers portal pressure and lowers mortality in the setting of acute variceal bleeding.
option_C: With optimal management his risk of mortality from this admission would be below 40%
claude_rationale: Proton pump inhibitors are not indicated in acute variceal bleeding as varices are venous structures unrelated to acid peptic disease. Terlipressin reduces portal pressure and mortality, antibiotics prevent SBP, and mortality with optimal care is typically 15-20%.

stem: A 60-year-old female complains of dry mouth and a gritty sensation in her eyes. She states it is sometimes difficult to speak for more than a few minutes. There is no history of diabetes mellitus or neurologic disease. The patient is on no medications. On exam, the buccal mucosa appears dry and the salivary glands are enlarged bilaterally. Which of the following is the best next step in evaluation?
option_B: Schirmer test and measurement of autoantibodies
claude_rationale: Clinical presentation strongly suggests Sjögren's syndrome with sicca symptoms (dry mouth, dry eyes) and salivary gland enlargement. Schirmer test objectively measures tear production and autoantibodies (anti-Ro/SSA, anti-La/SSB, RF) are key diagnostic markers for Sjögren's syndrome.

stem: A 79-year-old man has had abdominal pain for 4 days. An operation is performed, and a gangrenous appendix is removed. Why does acute appendicitis in elderly patients and in children have a worse prognosis?
option_B: The omentum and peritoneal cavity appear to be less efficient in localizing the disease in these age groups.
option_C: There may be a delay in establishing the right diagnosis in these age groups.
option_D: The appendix is in the pelvic position.
claude_rationale: Elderly patients and children often present with atypical symptoms of appendicitis, leading to diagnostic delays. This delayed diagnosis results in progression to complications like perforation and gangrene before treatment.

stem: A 36-year-old woman who is breastfeeding her first child complains of a smooth, mobile, painless lump in the outer quadrant of the right breast. Core biopsy is attempted and a white opaque fluid is expressed. The most likely diagnosis in this patient is:
claude_rationale: A smooth, mobile, painless lump in a breastfeeding woman that expresses white opaque fluid on biopsy is classic for galactocele. The fluid expression during core biopsy is pathognomonic for this milk-filled cyst.

stem: A 10-year-old boy presents with a history of sore throat three days ago. He now has a severe sore throat, only on the right side, with a fever of 38.5 C and trismus. On your exam, you can see soft palate swelling, most notably on the right, and the uvula is deviated to the left. There is edema and erythema of the uvula. Which of the following is the correct diagnosis?
claude_rationale: The clinical presentation describes peritonsillar abscess with unilateral throat pain, trismus, soft palate swelling, and uvular deviation away from the affected side. This diagnosis is not listed among the available options.

stem: A 27-year-old female presents with palpitations, weight loss, polyuria, diarrhea, and increased appetite for 3 weeks. Her past medical history is non-significant. Which of the following investigations is the most appropriate for this patient?
claude_rationale: The constellation of symptoms - palpitations, weight loss despite increased appetite, polyuria, and diarrhea - strongly suggests hyperthyroidism. Thyroid function tests would be the most appropriate initial investigation to confirm this diagnosis.

stem: A 40-year-old female complains of 7 weeks of pain and swelling in both wrists and knees. The patient complained of fatigue and lethargy several weeks before noticing the joint pain. The patient notes that after a period of rest, resistance to movement is more striking. On exam, the metacarpophalangeal joints and wrists are warm and tender. There are no other joint abnormalities. There is no alopecia, photosensitivity, kidney disease, or rash. Which of the following is correct?
option_A: The clinical picture suggests early rheumatoid arthritis, and a rheumatoid factor should be obtained
option_B: The prodrome of lethargy suggests chronic fatigue syndrome
option_C: Lack of systemic symptoms suggests osteoarthritis
option_D: X-rays of the hand are likely to show joint space narrowing and erosion
claude_rationale: The presentation of symmetric polyarthritis affecting MCPs and wrists with morning stiffness, fatigue prodrome, and warmth/tenderness strongly suggests early rheumatoid arthritis. X-rays would likely be normal in early disease, making option D incorrect.

stem:
The mini mental status examination (MMSE) named after Folstein is a brief screening tool to confirm the presence of cognitive impairment and to follow progression of dementia. 
The scoring is done out of?
claude_rationale: The MMSE is scored out of 30 points total, but this option is not provided in the choices given.

stem: A 45-year-old male presents with a daily headache. He describes two attacks per day over the last 3 weeks. Each attack lasts about an hour and awakens the patient from sleep. The patient has noted associated tearing and reddening of the right eye as well as nasal stuffiness. The pain is deep, excruciating, and limited to the right side of the head. The neurologic examination is non-focal. The most likely diagnosis of this patient’s headache is?
claude_rationale: The clinical presentation strongly suggests cluster headache: unilateral excruciating pain, autonomic symptoms (tearing, nasal congestion), nocturnal awakening, and stereotyped timing. None of the provided options include cluster headache, which is the most likely diagnosis.

stem: A 35-year-old nulliparous woman at 36 weeks' gestation was diagnosed with pre eclampsis She complains of headaches and visual disturbances. Her blood pressure is 150/100 mmHg and Hb 10 g/dl, platelets 90, ALT 88. She is at risk of the following complications except which one?
claude_rationale: Placenta praevia is a placental position abnormality unrelated to preeclampsia. The other options (cerebral hemorrhage, pulmonary edema, and eclampsia) are all direct complications of severe preeclampsia with the patient's concerning symptoms and lab values.

stem: Hawa has a fear of spiders and sees a therapist for help. The therapist first teaches her deep relaxation breathing. Then she exposed Hawa to increasing grades of stimulus; a cartoon of a spider, a picture of a spider, a toy spider and then a real spider. She encourages and helps Hawa to practice the deep breathing and feel relaxed at each grade before moving to the next. What technique is the therapist using?
claude_rationale: This describes systematic desensitization using graded exposure, where the patient is gradually exposed to increasingly anxiety-provoking stimuli while maintaining relaxation. The key elements are the hierarchical progression from cartoon to real spider and pairing each step with relaxation techniques.

stem:
A patient has 5 days of vomiting and generalized, colicky pain. Radiological examination is consistent with your diagnosis of small bowel obstruction. The blood work shows the following: WBC is 12.0, Cr 2.0 (normal <1.1), Na 130, K 2.5, Cl 90 BUN 18. What is your next step in management? 
 

 
.
option_A: Immediate surgery as she has confirmed bowel obstruction with an elevated WBC and renal failure and likely gangrenous bowel and continue resuscitation post-operatively.
option_B: Immediate resuscitation with 2.0 L bolus of normal saline, IV K replacement with 20 MeQ bolus immediately, and a blood transfusion if HGB is below normal to minimize fluid required and then consider surgery.
option_C: Immediate resuscitation with normal saline or ringer’s lactate in 1.0L boluses until urine output established K replacement after urine output confirmed, check Hgb level and cross-match for possible transfusion and then consider surgery.
option_D: Fluid boluses of 2.0L or more of normal saline with concurrent vasopressor treatment to maintain normal blood pressure to maintain kidney perfusion as she is in renal failure and severely dehydrated and then consider surgery
claude_rationale: Patient has severe dehydration with acute kidney injury and hypokalemia from small bowel obstruction. Safe resuscitation requires establishing urine output before potassium replacement to avoid hyperkalemia, and systematic fluid resuscitation before surgical consideration.

stem: A 32-year-old male with no medical conditions presents with generalized muscle aches and passage of progressively darkening urine one day after running the marathon. Physical examination is unremarkable aside mild tenderness over the quadriceps. Urine dipstick shows blood 3+ and leukocytes 1+. What is the most likely diagnosis?
claude_rationale: Marathon running causing muscle aches, dark urine, and 3+ blood on dipstick without RBCs suggests rhabdomyolysis with myoglobinuria. Intravascular hemolysis from mechanical trauma during prolonged running can also cause hemoglobinuria and dark urine.

stem: 64.9. A 52-year-old patient presents to your office with a left 4 cm histologically proven left breast carcinoma. On examination, she has a hard discrete mobile left axillary lymph node. A biopsy of a left supraclavicular node is positive for malignancy. There is no imaging evidence of distal metastasis. What is the TNM stage of the disease?
claude_rationale: A 4cm tumor is T2, and positive supraclavicular lymph node is N3, with no distant metastasis being M0. The correct staging should be T2N3M0, which is not among the provided options.

stem: A 69-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department after a syncopal attack while shopping at the market. She has recently completed a two-week course of amoxicillin, omeprazole and clarithromycin for eradication of Helicobacter pylori. His wife also mentioned he has had diarrhoea for the past few days. Blood pressure is 90/66 mmHg and heart rate is regular at 98 bpm. Electrocardiogram done showed a prolonged corrected QT interval of 540 ms. While being monitored, a short run of polymorphic ventricular tachycardia is seen on the cardiac monitor. What is the best intervention for this patient’s arrhythmia?
claude_rationale: Patient has prolonged QT interval (540ms) with polymorphic ventricular tachycardia consistent with torsades de pointes. Magnesium sulphate is the first-line treatment for torsades de pointes regardless of serum magnesium levels.

stem: 32. An 70 kg 28-year-old body builder reported to the ER with a 3-day history of intermittent colicky abdominal pain, total constipation, abdominal distension and 5 episodes of bilious vomiting. He also has a right groin swelling which had always been reducible until 3 days ago. He appears to be moderately dehydrated on examination. An NG passed yielded about a litre of bilious gastric fluid. Patient is being kept NPO as part of treatment, what is most appropriate fluid to use in the resuscitation of this patient.
claude_rationale: This patient has incarcerated inguinal hernia with bowel obstruction and significant gastric losses (1L bilious fluid). Normal saline is preferred for resuscitation when there are large upper GI losses due to high chloride content matching gastric secretions.

stem: A 68-year-old man is admitted to an acute psychiatric unit for severe suicidal ideation. He is very much preoccupied with death and refuses to agree to a contract for safety. The diagnostician determines the patient to be severely depressed because of noncompliance with medication and severe social stressors. The patient refuses to take any medication because, he says, “Nothing will change, anyway.” He also stops eating and drinking and becomes increasingly dehydrated. A reasonable choice of treatment in this patient would be to
option_A: Persuade the patient to take antidepressants
option_B: Wait and watch for the patient to change his mind
option_C: Restrain the patient and administer intravenous fluids
claude_rationale: This patient has severe depression with suicidal ideation, medication refusal, and life-threatening dehydration from refusing food/fluids. ECT is indicated for severe depression when patients refuse treatment and pose imminent danger to themselves.

stem: A 60-year-old male with significant history of smoking presents with a 4 month history of epigastric pain with associated jaundice, weight loss and loss of appetite. His abdomen is soft but there is epigastric tenderness. An abdominal ultrasound showed a mass in the head of the pancreas. Which tumor marker is most likely to be elevated?
claude_rationale: The clinical presentation strongly suggests pancreatic adenocarcinoma (epigastric pain, jaundice, weight loss, pancreatic head mass in a smoker). CEA is the most commonly elevated tumor marker in pancreatic cancer among the given options.

stem: A 52-year-old man is hospitalized after a severe myocardial infarction. On the second day in hospital when his physician comes by on rounds the patient insists on jumping out of bed and doing several pushups to show the physician that ‘they can’t keep a good man down-there is nothing wrong with me!’ Which defense mechanism BEST DESCRIBES this patient’s symptoms?
claude_rationale: The patient is refusing to acknowledge the reality of his severe myocardial infarction and its implications by insisting nothing is wrong and attempting physically demanding activity. This represents classic denial, where the patient unconsciously refuses to accept an unpleasant reality.

stem: You are the house officer on duty when a patient reports with complaint of hearing voices commenting on his every action even though he doesn’t see anyone talking. You rightly determine that this person is experiencing
claude_rationale: The patient describes third-person auditory hallucinations (voices commenting on actions), but this option is not provided. The given options describe passivity phenomena or second-person hallucinations, which don't match the clinical presentation.

stem: A 29-year-old woman attends A&E with a tender, swollen left breast. Her temperature is 38°C. She is two weeks post-partum and is breastfeeding. Ultrasound shows a cystic mass in the left breast. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
claude_rationale: This presentation is consistent with a breast abscess in a lactating woman. Needle aspiration is the appropriate first-line treatment for a cystic collection shown on ultrasound, combined with antibiotics.

stem: A 4 weeks old baby boy presents with a history of abdominal swelling and difficulty in passing urine. On evaluation and radiological examination, the baby is found to have posterior urethral valves. The parents seek to know the available treatment options from the doctor. All of the following are true except:
option_A: Emergency draining of the urine with a stent
option_B: Abrasion of the posterior urethral valves when the baby is stable
option_C: Vesicostomy is indicated when the baby is stable
option_D: Continuous monitoring of renal function is necessary
claude_rationale: Vesicostomy is typically performed when the baby is unstable or as an emergency procedure, not when stable. When stable, definitive valve ablation is preferred over vesicostomy.

stem: A 67-year-old woman with a 25-year smoking history presents to the clinic expressing concerns regarding an increase in the size of her right breast over the last 4 months. On examination, you find a non-tender, mobile, lobulated 10 cm mass with relatively smooth edges in the right breast. The right breast is significantly larger than the left and has a 'teardrop' appearance. The most likely diagnosis here is:
claude_rationale: The large, mobile, lobulated mass with smooth edges and teardrop appearance of the breast is characteristic of a lipoma. The non-tender, mobile nature and smooth edges make malignancy less likely, while the size and appearance are inconsistent with fibroadenoma or abscess.

stem:
A 20-year-old woman is seen in clinic because of a thyroid nodule. She is asymptomatic and her past medical history is unremarkable. She takes no medications. There is a 1-cm fi rm, solitary, nodule in
the lateral aspect of the left lobe of the thyroid. A radionuclide scan showed no uptake of tracer in the nodule. Ultrasonography shows a solid, homogenous 1-cm mass. Fine-needle aspiration cytology
shows a follicular neoplasm. What is the next best step in management?
option_C: Incisional biopsy and enucleation if benign
claude_rationale: Follicular neoplasm on FNA requires surgical excision to distinguish between follicular adenoma and carcinoma, as cytology cannot differentiate between them. Cold nodule with solid characteristics in a young patient warrants definitive surgical management.

stem: A 36 year-old woman presents with vaginal bleeding at 8 weeks 3 days’ gestation. She has never been pregnant before. The bright red ’spotting’ started 3 days ago with no abdominal pain. Vaginal examination revealed a closed cervix. Which of the following is helpful in the diagnosis of this condition?
claude_rationale: This presentation suggests threatened abortion (bleeding with closed cervix in first trimester). Transvaginal ultrasound is the most helpful diagnostic tool to assess fetal viability, gestational age, and rule out other causes of bleeding.

stem: One day after unprotected casual sex encounter with a man recently diagnosed as HIV infected, a young lady undergoes an antibody-based HIV test that returns negative. What is the earliest time to reschedule a repeat HIV antibody test;
claude_rationale: The window period for antibody-based HIV tests is typically 3-12 weeks, with most infections detectable by 3-4 weeks. Given the high-risk exposure, retesting at 3 weeks provides a reasonable balance between early detection and test reliability.

stem: A 6-day old infant delivered at 31 weeks who has been hospitalized from birth develops recurrent generalized seizures without fever. Lumbar puncture yields a uniformly bloody cerebrospinal fluid. The most likely aetiological factor is:
claude_rationale: Uniformly bloody CSF in a premature infant with seizures strongly indicates subarachnoid hemorrhage. Febrile seizures are excluded by absence of fever, and anencephaly would be diagnosed at birth.

stem: A 50-year-old man who was diagnosed of hypertension and started on Lisinopril 10 mg daily is referred to the Renal clinic due to hyperkalaemia and marked elevation in serum creatinine seen on on repeat kidney function test done after 2 weeks. What is the most useful investigation to identify the possible reason for this observation?
claude_rationale: ACE inhibitor-induced hyperkalemia and acute kidney injury in a newly diagnosed hypertensive patient suggests possible renal artery stenosis. CT renal angiogram would identify renovascular disease as the underlying cause.

stem: 11. A 45-year-old man who works on a cattle farm sustained an open injury of the foot after stepping on a nail that pierced through the boot. He was nursing the wound at home with an antiseptic solution. 2 weeks later she started having trouble moving his jaw. His back and neck became stiff and painful. He had worsening dysphagia and hesitancy on urination. He was rushed to the emergency of a local hospital after experiencing a seizure. What is the most like diagnosis of this patient
claude_rationale: The clinical presentation (puncture wound, jaw stiffness, neck/back rigidity, dysphagia, seizure) strongly suggests tetanus, which is not listed among the options. The timeline and symptoms are classic for tetanus following a contaminated puncture wound.

stem: A 30 year old female presents with weakness of the lower limbs. On examination there is loss of proprioception and vibration sense. Here full blood count reveals increased mean corpuscular volume (MCV). What is the likely diagnosis?
option_C: Subacute combined degeneration of the cord
claude_rationale: The combination of lower limb weakness, loss of proprioception and vibration sense (posterior column dysfunction), and macrocytic anemia (increased MCV) is classic for subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord due to B12 deficiency.

stem:
A 22-year-old patient with known rheumatic disease of the mitral
valve presents to thehospital with acute-onset pain in her right lower extremity. The limb is cool, and she describes a sensation of numbness and tingling. You are unable to appreciate pulses in the limb. Which of the following is TRUE of her management?
option_A: CTA should be performed following the initiation of a  heparin drip to confirm thediagnosis and guide the surgical  approach
option_B: This condition is treated with anticoagulation and observation.
option_C: The patient should undergo amputation of the limb following the initiation of a heparindrip
option_D: Emergent embolectomy is indicated following the initiation of a heparin drip
claude_rationale: This patient presents with acute limb ischemia (cool limb, numbness, absent pulses) likely due to embolic event from rheumatic mitral valve disease. Emergent surgical revascularization is required to salvage the limb, with heparin initiated to prevent further thrombosis.

stem: A middle age man presents with acute onset ascending paralysis. He recounts that he had suffered a preceding diarrhoeal illness which he recovered from. Examination reveals flaccid weakness of both lower limbs. Which organism is most likely associated with this presentation?
claude_rationale: This presentation is classic for Guillain-Barré syndrome with preceding diarrheal illness and acute ascending flaccid paralysis. Campylobacter jejuni is the most common infectious trigger for Guillain-Barré syndrome, particularly following gastroenteritis.

stem: A 60 year old woman presents with slurred speech and weakness involving the left side of the body. There is facial drooping to the opposite side of the body. On examination, tone on the affected side is increased with brisk reflexes. What is the likely arterial territory for this presentation?
claude_rationale: Left-sided body weakness with right facial drooping indicates a right-sided brain lesion due to crossed innervation of facial muscles. The combination of hemiparesis, facial involvement, and speech difficulties localizes to the right middle cerebral artery territory.

stem: Lupus pernio is the term for chronic sarcoidosis-related indurated, lumpy, violaceous, blue-purple lesions. It can be disfiguring by eroding into underlying cartilage & bone. The lesions are classically located on?
claude_rationale: Lupus pernio classically affects the nose, cheeks, ears, and fingers. The nose and cheeks are the most characteristic locations for these chronic sarcoidosis lesions.

stem: A 30-year-old male complains of leg weakness and paresthesias of the arm and leg. Five years previously he had an episode of transient visual loss. On physical exam, there is hyperreflexia, bilateral Babinski signs, and cerebellar dysmetria with poor finger-to-nose movement. When the patient is asked to look to the right, the left eye does not move normally past the midline. Nystagmus is noted in the abducting eye. A more detailed history suggests the patient has had several episodes of gait difficulty that have resolved spontaneously. He appears to be stable between these episodes. He has no systemic symptoms of fever or weight loss. Which of the following is the most appropriate next test to order?
claude_rationale: Clinical presentation strongly suggests multiple sclerosis with relapsing-remitting course, internuclear ophthalmoplegia, and cerebellar signs. MRI is the most appropriate initial diagnostic test to identify demyelinating lesions and assess disease burden.

stem: A lady who is 4 months pregnant presents with some pain in her right knee with a swelling that has been increasing slowly in the previous 2 months. There is only a slight decrease in flexion but not much added pain. A radiolucent lesion is noted in the subchondral region of the distal femur.
option_A: It is important to counsel the lady about the possible amputation of the affected femur
option_B: Nothing should be done until the lady delivers her baby
option_C: Urgent surgery of the lesion should be considered
option_D: The patient must be prepared for chemotherapy once her baby is delivered
claude_rationale: A radiolucent lesion in a pregnant woman could represent various conditions from benign to malignant. Without histological diagnosis, imaging characteristics, or additional clinical details, it's impossible to determine appropriate management timing or urgency.

stem: Iron released from enterocytes basolateral membrane binds to free sites on the plasma iron transport protein transferrin. Normal transferrin saturation is ~30%. Systemic regulation of iron absorption is mediated by?
claude_rationale: Ferroportin is the only iron exporter from enterocytes and is directly regulated by hepcidin, which is the master regulator of systemic iron homeostasis. When iron stores are high, hepcidin increases and causes ferroportin degradation, reducing iron absorption.

stem:
A woman had an emergency caesarean section for a pathological CTG and pyrexiain labour She was discharged on postoperative day 4 but re-admitted on day 
6 with pyrexia, tachypnoea, tachycardia and hypotension. Haemoglobin is 105 g/l. Septic shock is the main differential diagnosis. Following the Sepsis 6 bundle, along with antibiotics and blood cultures, which other important blood test needs to be taken?
claude_rationale: Lactate is a key component of the Sepsis 6 bundle for assessing tissue perfusion and severity of septic shock. Elevated lactate indicates inadequate tissue oxygenation and helps guide resuscitation efforts.

stem: Two hundred children with severe ITP were enrolled in a randomized clinical trial to compare the rate of nose bleeds between children who received anti D therapy and those who receive no treatment. One hundred were assigned to each group. Which of the following is the most appropriate statistical procedure?
claude_rationale: This study compares the rate (proportion) of nosebleeds between two independent groups. Chi-square test is appropriate for comparing categorical outcomes between independent groups.

stem:
A 50 year old woman complained of hot flushes, night sweats,
               decreased libido and absence of menses for 2 years duration. Her FSH
               was elevated at 52 i. u/L . The most likely diagnosis will be?
claude_rationale: The constellation of vasomotor symptoms (hot flushes, night sweats), decreased libido, amenorrhea for 2 years, and elevated FSH (52 IU/L) in a 50-year-old woman is classic for menopause. The elevated FSH indicates ovarian failure consistent with natural menopause.

stem: A 60-year-old male presents with complaints of dysphagia and a 6-kg weightloss. He states that four weeks ago, he noticed a 2 cm hard mass in the upperneck (Level II on your exam). You see an ulcerated mass in the left pyriform sinus on indirect laryngoscopy. The best next step to confirm the diagnosis should be:
option_A: An open biopsy of the neck mass
option_D: A CT scan of the neck and chest.
claude_rationale: CT scan is essential for staging suspected head and neck malignancy, evaluating extent of disease, and guiding biopsy approach. The clinical presentation strongly suggests pyriform sinus carcinoma with nodal metastasis requiring comprehensive imaging before tissue sampling.

stem: You are in the emergency department and you meet one of your patients from hypertensive clinic coming in with complaints of shortness of breath at rest, lower limb swelling and a cough. You immediately think it could be heart failure and you decide to do a chest Xray. Which one of the following would be suggestive of heart failure?
claude_rationale: All four options (pleural effusion, dilated upper lobe vessels, Kerley B lines, and cardiomegaly) are classic radiographic findings suggestive of heart failure. The question asks for which ONE finding would be suggestive, but doesn't specify which is most characteristic or earliest.

stem: A 37-year-old factory worker develops increasing weakness in the legs; coworkers have noted episodes of transient confusion. The patient has bilateral foot drop and atrophy; mild wrist weakness is also present. His CBC shows an anemia with a hemoglobin of 9.6 g/dL ; examination of the peripheral blood smear shows basophilic stippling. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient’s symptoms?
claude_rationale: The combination of peripheral neuropathy with bilateral foot drop, anemia, and basophilic stippling on blood smear is classic for lead poisoning. The occupational exposure as a factory worker and episodes of confusion further support this diagnosis.

stem: A 52-year-old otherwise healthy man presents to the emergency room with complaints of acute-onset pain and heaviness in his right leg. He has no pulsesin the right lower extremity. His heart rate is 153 and irregular. What is the most likely cause of this presentation?
option_A: Aortic dissection that has progressed to the iliofemoral level
option_C: Thrombotic complication of atherosclerotic plaque of the femoral artery
claude_rationale: The presentation of acute limb ischemia with absent pulses plus irregular tachycardia strongly suggests atrial fibrillation with arterial embolism. None of the given options include embolic arterial occlusion, which is the most likely diagnosis.

stem: A 57 year old known hypertensive who is poorly controlled presents with sudden onset headache which he describes as the worst headache of his life. You suspect he might be having a subarachnoid hemorrhage. A CT scan is unavailable to you. What is the ideal time to do a lumbar puncture to confirm your suspicion?
claude_rationale: Lumbar puncture for suspected SAH should be performed after 12 hours of symptom onset to allow sufficient time for red blood cell breakdown and xanthochromia development, which helps distinguish true SAH from traumatic tap.

stem: A 78-year-old man with diabetes mellitus presents with fever, headache and altered sensorium. On physical exam his temperature is 40.2°C, heart rate is 103 beats/min, blood pressure is 84/52 mmHg. His neck is stiff and he has photophobia. His cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) examination shows 2100 cells/µL, with 100% neutrophils, glucose 0.6 mmol/L , and protein 0.78 g/dL . CSF gram stain is negative. Empirical therapy should include which of the following?
claude_rationale: This presents as bacterial meningitis with classic CSF findings (high neutrophils, low glucose, elevated protein) despite negative gram stain. Dexamethasone should be given before or with the first dose of antibiotics to reduce neurological complications, particularly in elderly patients.

stem: A 78-year-old retired plumber presented to the general clinic complaining of gradually worsening difficulty breathing and dull left sided chest pain for the past 4 months. He has a 10-pack year history of smoking. Chest examination findings were reduced chest expansion, stony dull percussion note and reduced breath sounds all on the left. Chest X ray done showed a large left sided pleural effusion and pleural plaques. What is the most likely cause of the pleural effusion?
